coredata.dc:title: The antibody assisted detection of HER2 on a cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework and gold functionalized graphene quantum dots modified electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Centane, Sixolile
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: In this work a novel simple, inexpensive and highly sensitive approach for the antibody assisted detection of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) is presented. Three sensors using carboxylic group containing gold functionalized graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs@AuNPs), CoP-BNF (cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework) and CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (the conjugate) nanocomposites on a GCE were designed. The sensors were fabricated by covalent immobilization of the antibody to the GCE via an amide bond. The modified GCE was characterized using cyclic voltammetry and impedance spectroscopy. The performance of the sensors was assessed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The selective recognition of the antibody towards HER2 on the electrode led to an increase of the electron transfer resistance (Rct). The different modified electrodes had low detection limit values: 0.0327 ng/mL, 0.0454 ng/mL and 0.1072 ng/mL towards the detection of HER2. The sensors showed good stability and reproducibility. The sensors showed promising potential in clinical applicability, in the detection of HER2 protein in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework, Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2
Graphene quantum dots
Impedance spectroscopy
Trastuzumab

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework (CoP-BNF), Scheme 1
Synthesis of the gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) functionalized SNGQDs (SNGQDs@AuNPs), Scheme 2
Cyclic voltammetry characterization of the fabricated Immunosensor
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) characterization of the fabricated Immunosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Republic of South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ), and Rhodes University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of (A) CoP-BNF (a) and CoP (b), and (B) SNGQDs alone (a) and SNGQDs@AuNPs (b) all in water.
FT-IR spectroscopy of (A) H2P (a) and CoP-BNF(b), and (B) SNGQDs alone (a) and SNGQDs@AuNPs (b).
XRD data for (A) the mononuclear metallated CoP and the CoP-BNF, and (B) SNGQDs (a) and the gold decorated SNGQDs@AuNPs (b).
TEM images obtained for SNGQDs alone (left) and SNGQDs@AuNPs (right).
Raman Spectroscopy data for the SNGQDs alone (a) and the gold nanoparticle decorated SNGQDs@AuNPs (b). All samples were run in water.

Cyclic voltammograms of A: bare GCE (a), GCE/CoP-BNF (b), GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (c), GCE/SNGQDs (d), GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs (e), and B: GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab (blue dotted line) and CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab /HER2 (blue solid line) modified electrodes in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4). HER2 concentration = 5 ng/mL.

Nyquist plots of various electrodes; BGCE (a), GCE/CoP-BNF (b), GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (c), GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab (d) and GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab /HER2 (e). All measurements were performed 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4). HER2 concentration = 4 ng/mL.
SEM images of the modified electrode surface depicting the various steps towards biosensor fabrication; BGCE (a), GCE/CoP-BNF (b) and GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (c).

EIS data at various concentrations of HER2 using (A) GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trasmatuzab and (B) GCE/CoP-BNF/Trasmatuzab. Alongside (right) regression plots, Ret vs log [HER2]. All in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4).

Nyquist plots for EIS response of the fresh and regenerated immunosensor; (A) HER2 decoupled (blue plots) and (B) HER2 coupled (black plots), three cycles each. HER2 concentration = 10 ng/mL. All in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4).
Synthetic route towards the synthesis of the cobalt porphyrin based organic framework (CoP-BNF).
Synthetic approach from SNGQDs to SNGQDs@AuNPs.

Schematic representation of the antibody-based assay for the detection of HER2: modification of the glassy carbon electrode (a) with CoP-BNF (b) and SNGQDs@AuNPs (c). Activation of the porphyrin and SNGQDs@AuNPs carboxylic groups using EDC/NHS coupling and immobilization of Trastuzumab (d), blocking step with BSA in 0.1 M PBS (e) and (f) reaction with the HER2 protein.
Electrochemical data in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4).
Comparison of analytical performance of electrochemical sensors for HER2 detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CV = Cyclic voltammetry, LSV = linear sweep voltammetry, EIS = electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. b SPE = Screen Printed Electrode, SPCE = Screen Printed Carbon Electrode, AuNPs = gold nanoparticles, CILE = carbon ionic liquid electrode, MW = multiwall carbon nanotubes, GE = gold electrode, GNPE = gold nanoparticles, MPA = 3-mercaptopropionic acid, Cyst = cysteamine, CdSe = cadmium sulfide quantum dots, ZnS = Zinc sulfide quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reproducibility data (%RSD) and LOD in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4).
Recovery measurements of HER2 spiked in human serum samples for the GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs.

head.ce:title: The antibody assisted detection of HER2 on a cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework and gold functionalized graphene quantum dots modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Chemistry Department, PO Box 94, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Chemistry Department, PO Box 94, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work a novel simple, inexpensive and highly sensitive approach for the antibody assisted detection of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) is presented. Three sensors using carboxylic group containing gold functionalized graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs@AuNPs), CoP-BNF (cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework) and CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (the conjugate) nanocomposites on a GCE were designed. The sensors were fabricated by covalent immobilization of the antibody to the GCE via an amide bond. The modified GCE was characterized using cyclic voltammetry and impedance spectroscopy. The performance of the sensors was assessed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The selective recognition of the antibody towards HER2 on the electrode led to an increase of the electron transfer resistance (Rct). The different modified electrodes had low detection limit values: 0.0327 ng/mL, 0.0454 ng/mL and 0.1072 ng/mL towards the detection of HER2. The sensors showed good stability and reproducibility. The sensors showed promising potential in clinical applicability, in the detection of HER2 protein in human serum.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework, Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2

ce:section.ce:para: The major drawback concerning successful cancer treatment is timing in detection [1]. As a result, the development of highly sensitive, non-invasive and low cost immunosensors for the detection and monitoring of cancer markers remain a major area of research [2,3]. Compared to the conventional detection methods (e.g. mammography), immunosensors are preferred since they are non-invasive and function by analysis of tumour biomarkers in biological fluids of patients such as serum, plasma, blood, etc. [4,5]. Tumour biomarkers can be defined as molecules or processes indicative of the presence of cancer in the body or the likelihood of progression or response to therapy [6,7]. Human epidermal growth factor receptor (HER2) is an established cancer biomarker, and it is over-expressed in 20–30% of breast cancer cases [8,9]. Other cancers such as ovarian, stomach, pancreatic and prostate have also been associated with HER2 [10,11]. Current HER2 detection methods include fluorescent in situ hybridisation (FISH) or immunohistochemistry (IHC) which involve time consuming steps and are invasive [12,13]. In this work we propose the detection of HER2 using a cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework and sulfur/nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots functionalized with gold nanoparticles. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are well known carbon based nanomaterials, with properties similar to those of graphene [14]. GQDs based nanocarrier labelled with Trastuzumab (anti-HER2) and β-cyclodextrin has been employed for the detection of HER2 [15]. The immobilization of Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) onto carbon support (GQDs in our case) has been reported to prevent the aggregation of the latter as well as to enhance the detection sensitivity of analytes [16]. Hence, GQDs are used in combination with AuNPs and Co porphyrin binuclear framework (CoP-BNF) in this work. Sulfur/nitrogen doped GQDs (SNGQDs) are employed since the introduction of S and N into GQDs causes the synergistic effect of N and S, which facilitates the electron transfer, hence improving electrocatalysis [17].

Porphyrins are aromatic compounds which are electroactive [18], and have good structural flexibility. Their properties can be fine-tuned to suit the desired application [19]. Polymeric porphyrins are known for improved electrocatalytic activity [20]. A porphyrin-based covalent organic framework has been used to immobilize aptamer strands for the detection of epidermal growth factor receptor [21]. In another study, multiwalled carbon nanotubes together with an anionic porphyrin, were used to immobilized DNA fragment specific to HER2, hence used in the quantification of HER2 [22]. In this work we use for the first time Trastuzumab to detect HER2 when immobilized on CoP-BNF. We use Trastuzumab since it has been approved as a first line for treatment of HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer [23]. Even though porphyrin organic frameworks have been employed for HER2 detection [21], in this work we employ a simpler binuclear porphyrin, which has been reported to preserve the networking of the former [24]. To the best of our knowledge we report for the first time on the antibody assisted detection of HER2, using SNGQDs@AuNPs with binuclear cobalt porphyrin.

The following chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich: hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) tetrahydrate, trisodium citrate, potassium hydroxide, cobalt (acetate)3, ethanol, pyridine, bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, K4[Fe(CN)6], K3[Fe(CN)6], human serum (AB male plasma), the protein (HER2), and the monoclonal antibody HER2 (Trastuzumab). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared using weighed amounts of Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 and NaCl from Type II water which was obtained from an Elga PURELAB Chorus 2 (RO/DI) system. N, N dimethyl formamide (DMF) was purchased from Merck. The free base (H2P) [25] and Co(II) [26] tetracarboxyphenyl porphyrins (CoP) were synthesized as outlined in literature. The synthesis of SNGQDs has also been reported [27,28].

Ground state electronic absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx-Eye Detector, using Cu Ka radiation (= 1.5405 Å, nickel filter). The morphologies of nanoparticles were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80 kV. The surface of the modified electrodes was assessed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), the TESCAN Vega TS 5136LM model. Glassy carbon plates (GCP, Goodfellow, UK) of 1 × 1 cm and 2 mm thick were used as a substrate for SEM. Mass spectral data were obtained on a Bruker Auto FLEX III Smart-beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer, using a-cyano-4-hydrocinnamic acid as the matrix in the positive ion mode. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman spectral data. The elemental analysis was conducted using a Vario-Elementar Microcube ELIII.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies were performed using an Autolab Potentiostat PGSTAT30 equipped with Nova software version 2.1.

The EIS experiments were performed between 0.1 and 10 kHz, using a 5 mV/rms sinusoidal modulation. A non-linear least squares (NLLS) method based on the EQUIVCRT programme was used for automatic fitting of the obtained EIS data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework (CoP-BNF), Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para: The cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework was synthesized following a literature method [24], Scheme 1 as follows: H2P (9.2 mg, 0.012 mmol) was dissolved in dimethylformamide (DMF, 2 mL), followed by addition of aqueous KOH (1 mL, 0.2 M) to assist in the deprotonation of some of the COOH groups in H2P. To this solution, Co (acetate)3 (8.6 mg/mL aq.) was added followed by pyridine (0.1 mL) to increase solubility. The contents were placed in a 400 mL Teflon lined autoclave and the reaction temperature was set at 150 °C for a period of 50 h, followed by cooling to room temperature. The resultant sample was centrifuged at 24000 rpm at 5 min intervals using ethanol to wash off any unreacted material. The binuclear framework was dried under vacuum overnight. Yield: 7.6 mg (82.6%), FT-IR: ν (-OH) 3434 cm−1, ν(c=o) 1797 cm−1 and ν(c=c) 1674 cm−1. UV–Vis (in H2O): λmax (log ε) nm Soret band: 419 (4.87), Q-band1: 544 (4.01), Q-band2: 580 (3.70). Elemental analysis for C98H57Co3N8O18 calc.: C, 64.98; H, 3.14; N, 6.18. Found: C, 63.02; H, 3.41; N, 5.98. MS (MALDI-TOF) (m/z): Calculated: 1811.34 Found: 1814.32 [M + 3H+].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) functionalized SNGQDs (SNGQDs@AuNPs), Scheme 2
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functionalization of the SNGQDs with gold nanoparticles was achieved following a seed growth method previously reported [29] as follows: SNGQDs (10 mL, 1 mg/mL in H2O) solution was sonicated for 10 min to ensure dissolution, followed by heating at 80 °C. To the heated SNGQDs solution, an aqueous solution of trisodium citrate (10 mL, 2.2 mM) was injected. Following that, a HAuCl4 (1 mL, 25 mM) aqueous solution was quickly added to the mixture of SNGQDs and sodium citrate. The temperature of the reaction was increased to 90 °C, and the solution was left to boil for a further 20 min before being cooled down to room temperature. Prior to rotatory evaporation, ethanol was added to the particles to dissolve any unreacted material. The particles (SNGQDs@AuNPs) were dried in an oven overnight.
Please note that GCP (for SEM) were modified using the same protocol described below for GCE.

The following method of antibody (Trastuzumab) immobilization was used: The GCE (a, Scheme 3) was thoroughly polished to a silver mirror on a Buehler-felt pad using alumina (<10 μm) and then washed with Millipore water. The electrode was then sonicated for 5 min in Millipore water. In between each polishing step, the electrode was washed with Millipore water to remove impurities. The electrode was modified using the drop and dry method as follows: firstly, CoP-BNF (2 mg, 0.00175 mmol in water) was added to the GCE (b, Scheme 3) and left to dry in the oven, represented as GCE/CoP-BNF, then SNGQDs@AuNPs (5 μL of 1 mg/mL) aqueous solution was added on top of the CoP-BNF layer forming a GCE-CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs electrode (c, Scheme 3). Electrodes where SNGQDs@AuNPs (5 μL of 1 mg/mL) aqueous solution was separately added to the GCE were also developed and left to dry in the oven, represented as GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs.

Both the CoP-BNF and the SNGQDs@AuNPs have carboxylic groups which were activated using the EDC/NHS coupling reaction as follows: The GCE-CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs, GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs and GCE/CoP-BNF were separately immersed in an aqueous solution of NHS (0.3 mL, 100 mM) and EDC (0.1 mL, 50 mM) and were stored for 2 h at 4 °C (d, Scheme 3, for GCE-CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs). The activation made it possible for covalent attachment of the Trastuzumab antibody (which has amino groups) onto the electrode surface via an amide bond as follows: Trastuzumab (10 μL) was added separately to GCE-CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs, GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs and GCE/CoP-BNF and the electrodes were left for 8 h at 4 °C to allow the interaction of CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (activated), GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs (activated) and GCE/CoP-BNF (activated) with Trastuzumab to form CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/ Trastuzumab (d, Scheme 3), GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab and GCE/CoP-BNF/Trastuzumab. Immunosensors on the electrodes GCE/CoP-BNF and GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs were prepared similarly in order to investigate the synergistic role of the CoP-BNF and SNGQDs. Thus there are three electrodes under investigation: GCE/CoP-BNF/Trastuzumab, GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab and GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab.

In order to limit non-specific binding on the free surface sites on the electrode, BSA (in 20 μL PBS) was added to the modified electrodes: GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab, GCE/CoP-BNF/Trastuzumab and GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab, followed by incubation for 2 h at 37 °C. The resulting electrodes (e, Scheme 3; showing GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab/BSA as an example) were then washed with PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), followed by rinsing with Millipore water. Finally, antibody (Trastuzumab) modified electrode with BSA blocking layer were ready for capturing the target analyte (HER2) (f, Scheme 3).

The prepared immunosensor assays were immersed in various concentrations of the HER2, followed by rinsing with PBS buffer to remove unbound protein. The studied HER2 concentrations ranged between 1 and 10 ng/L in PBS and the temperature was maintained at 37 °C for 60 min at each concentration.

The modified electrodes were characterized by EIS in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) at each stage of the modification. The potential was set at 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl. For EIS, the frequency range was from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz with perturbation amplitude of 10 mV.
Human serum (from male AB clotted whole blood) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The sample was stored at −20 °C until use. The supernatant was dissolved in PBS for experimental use.

The CoP-BNF was prepared by the hydrothermal technique, which involves reacting the metal free porphyrin (dissolved in DMF) and adding dilute aqueous potassium hydroxide solution to enhance deprotonation of the carboxylic groups of H2P, followed by addition of Co (acetate)3. The reaction occurs in a sealed reactor at 150 °C for about 50 h as reported before [24]. Tetracarboxy phenoxy porphyrin is known to assemble into various structures (such as polynuclear, tetranuclear, and binuclear) in the presence of guest atoms [24]. The CoP-BNF was synthesized as reported in literature resulting in the reported structure shown in Scheme 1, with the cobalt ion connecting to two different carboxylate groups (with 6 O atoms including two from the acetate ligand). Mass spectra confirmed the formation of a binuclear structure with expected mass of 1811.34 a.u and found 1814.32 a.u.

The UV–Vis spectroscopic spectra of cobalt tetracarboxyphenyl porphyrin (CoP) and cobalt porphyrin binuclear framework (CoP-BNF) are shown in Fig. 1A. The CoP-BNF showed a single Soret band at 419 nm with two Q bands at 544 nm and 580 nm (the latter weak) typical of metallated porphyrin molecules. The Soret band for CoP-BNF is observed at the same wavelength as CoP (419 nm). The FT-IR spectrum of the CoP-BNF is compared to that of an unmetallated tetracarboxyphenyl porphyrin (H2P), Fig. 2A. An unmetallated porphyrin has the inner ring –NH groups, which were confirmed by the peak at 3319 cm−1, Fig. 2A(a). In addition, an expected broad peak at 3349 cm−1 was observed, which is due to the –OH of the carboxylic groups in the porphyrin. Peaks at 1685 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1 associated with the -C=O stretch and -C=C stretch, respectively, were observed. Similar vibrational stretches were observed for the CoP-BNF. There is a shift of the broad carboxylic peak at 3349 cm−1 for H2P to 3434 cm−1 for CoP-BNF and the disappearance of the –NH stretch symbolizing successful metalation, Fig. 2B(b). The presence of the carboxylic acid groups (3434 cm−1) confirms that not all of them were used for the coordination of Co. The structure of the synthesized CoP-BNF was further explored using X-ray Diffraction (XRD), the results are shown in Fig. 3A. The CoP-BNF alone showed an XRD pattern similar to that of CoP with a broad peak at 2θ = 21°, confirming the amorphous in nature of the porphyrins. Additional weaker peaks were observed at 2θ = 42° (for CoP) and 2θ = 60° for CoP-BNF [30], in addition to some sharp peaks for the latter. The XRD patterns of CoP and CoP-BNF are basically similar showing that the π-conjugated structure is retained after the formation of CoP-BNF as observed before, Fig. 3A [30].

Scheme 2 shows the synthesis of SNGQDs@AuNPs. The well-known affinity of gold to S and N was exploited for the in-situ synthesis of the AuNPs onto SNGQDs, a process which merely involves their self-assembly onto the SNGQDs surface owing to the presence of sulfur and nitrogen groups in the as-synthesized GQDs. The successful fabrication of the final conjugates was confirmed by particle size analysis, X-ray diffraction, absorption, Raman spectral analysis.

The absorption spectrum of SNGQDs without the gold nanoparticles was typical of GQDs, Fig. 1B(a) with an absorption peak at 350 nm. The absorption spectrum of SNGQDs@AuNPs (Fig. 1B(b) has an extra peak at around 539 nm (the surface plasmon resonance, SPR, band) which is typical of gold nanoparticles, hence the adsorption of gold on the SNGQDs was confirmed. The structural composition of the synthesized nanomaterials was further explored using FT-IR spectroscopy, Fig. 2B. Broad peaks in the 3200–3500 cm−1 region were obtained for both SNGQDs (3400 cm−1) and SNGQDs@AuNPs (3298 cm−1) symbolic of the peripheral carboxylic and hydroxyl groups. The synthesized SNGQDs and SNGQDs@AuNPs both had bands in the 1400 cm−1- 1800 cm−1 region which were associated with vibrational stretch of the carbonyl (C=O) in COOH and O=C-NH (from cysteine as a starting material during synthesis). Two additional peaks at 1260 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 which are due to CS and CN bonds in the SNGQDs were observed, Fig. 2B(a). The peaks shift to 1249 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 and decrease drastically in intensity for SNGQDs@AuNPs, Fig. 2B(b). This might be due to the successful decoration of the SNGQDs with AuNPs, resulting in AuN and AuS bonds. The -OH stretch of the SNGQDs@AuNPs is intensified compared to that of the SNGQDs alone; this might be due to the sodium citrate used as reducing/coating agent which has alcohol groups. A significant shift in the functional groups mentioned, on the SNGQDs@AuNPs compared to the SNGQDs might be suggestive of structural changes and successful decoration on the SNGQDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the synthesized nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 3B. The SNGQDs showed a typical XRD pattern for graphene based materials with an amorphous carbon peak at 2θ = 18°. This peak was present in the pattern for the SNGQDs@AuNPs (but weak), with additional crystalline peaks corresponding to that of gold nanoparticles: {(111), (200), (220), (311), (222) and (400)} further confirming the presence of gold nanoparticles in the sample.

The size distribution of the SNGQDs and SNGQDs@AuNPs was studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images of SNGQDs and SNGQDs@AuNPs are displayed in Fig. 4 . The TEM images obtained showed that the particles maintain a spherical nature before (SNGQDs) and after modification (SNGQDs@AuNPs) respectively. A size increase is observed, moving from SNGQDs (2.8 nm) and SNGQDs@AuNPs (5.4 nm), suggestive of successful modification of SNGQDs surface. There is some aggregation for SNGQDs@AuNPs.

The Raman spectroscopy data obtained for the synthesized GQDs are shown in Fig. 5 . Graphene quantum dots are known to exhibit two characteristic Raman peaks termed the D-disorder (breathing mode, sp3) band and G (sp2, tangential mode) band. The synthesized SNGQDs had D-band and G-band occurring at 1297 cm−1 and 1597 cm−1, respectively. For the SNGQDs@AuNPs, there is a 10 unit shift in the D-band to 1287 cm−1, and by 7 units (to 1604 cm−1) for the G-band was observed. The surface functionalization of GQDs introduces defects onto the graphene framework. The defects are quantified by the ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG). The ID/IG ratio for SNGQDs and SNGQDs@AuNPs was found to be 1.38 and 0.85 respectively. The varied ratio might be due to the synthetic method for SNGQDs@AuNPs, which involved heating leading to the altering the GQD framework during decoration with AuNPs. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum of SNGQDs@AuNPs showed broadened D-band and some additional unidentifiable peaks. Appearance of many peaks in the Raman spectra of GQDs has been observed and was attributed to nanosized GQDs with large edge defects [31].

The antibody (Trastuzumab) was covalently linked to the carboxylic rich GQDs and porphyrin framework on the GCE. The carboxylic groups on both the GQDs and porphyrin framework were activated using the EDC/NHS coupling reaction. The representation of the different steps towards fabrication of the assay is shown in Scheme 3 (showing GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs as an example).

The immobilization of antibodies onto a transducer surface plays a significant role in biosensor development [32]. The affinity, orientation and stability of the antibody is highly dependent on the immobilization technique. Understanding the conformation and structure of the antibody, is key for determining the immobilization technique onto an electrode [33]. Antibodies in solution have been proven to have a 3D freedom of movement, which is minimized upon adsorption. In a case where the antibody is used as a capture molecule, it is important to understand the interactions which occur between the antibody and analyte to be captured [32,33]. An ideal technique for the immobilization of an antibody on an electrode surface is that which maintains the native conformation and immunoactivity, before and after immobilization as well as after capturing the desired analyte. In this work, the antibody (Trastuzumab) was linked onto GCE modified with a CoP-BNF and SNGQDs@AuNPs via amide linkage following the EDC/NHS coupling reaction. The CoP-BNF, is an ideal choice for enhancement of electron conductivity of the electrode. The same can be said for SNGQDs@AuNPs, where the addition of the AuNPs was used to minimize aggregation of the GQDs. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS, Nyquist plots) was used to monitor the performance of the immunosensor throughout the construction. The semicircle part of the Nyquist plots at higher frequencies is related to the electron transfer (ET) limited process, so it is possible to investigate the surface change at each step of the modification process by measuring the semicircle diameter which equals the ET resistance (Rct) [34].

EIS was employed for the optimization experiments. The HER2 immunosensor was optimized with respect to several experimental conditions such as antibody concentration and pH variation. The incubation time was kept at 60 min for HER2 immobilization. All experiments were conducted at 37 °C for Trastuzumab and HER2. Optimization of the amount of antibody and pH was conducted using EIS measurements and in 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox probe prepared in PBS buffer. The choice of the optimum antibody quantity was determined by incubation of the CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (activated) with various concentration of Trastuzumab overnight (1, 2, 3 and 4 mg/mL), (Fig. S1 in supporting information). The EIS response of the various antibody concentrations was recorded. It was observed that the magnitude of the Rct value increased with an increase in Trastuzumab concentration. To avoid over stacking of the antibody and competition of bonding sites on the electrode surface, the 1 mg/mL was used. The effect of pH on the modified electrodes was studied using EIS at pH ~ 6.4 to 7.4 (pH = 0.2 intervals) in 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in PBS buffer (Figs. S2, S3 in supporting information). The modified electrodes GCE/CoP-BNF and GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (in the absence of Trastuzumab, Figs. S2, S3) showed little to no change in Rct values at pH ~ 6.8 to 7.4, hence showing stability. The stability of HER2 and Trastuzumab at pH 7.4 has been widely reported in literature, and as such the experiments reported herein were conducted at that pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetry characterization of the fabricated Immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammograms (CV) were employed to characterize the surface alterations of the electrode following subsequent modification with SNGQDs@AuNPs, CoP-BNF, CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs, GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab and GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab /HER2 in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4 in PBS, pH 7.4, all shown in Fig. 6 . The CVs shows the ferricyanide peaks near 0.2 V. The peaks near 0.6 are due CoP based processes and occur only for CoP and CoP-BNF containing electrodes.

Regarding the ferricyanide peaks, the bare GCE (Fig. 6a) had anodic to cathodic peak separation (ΔEp) = 68 mV, (Table 1 ) which is close to the ideal Nernst value. Variations in peak separation was observed for the various nanomaterials, showing varying electron transfer properties. According to Table 1, CoP and CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs showed higher ΔEp values compared to the BGCE (bare glassy carbon electrode), this might be due to passivation of the electrode by the highly π-conjugated nanomaterials used which might undergo aggregation on the electrode surface. The introduction of BSA layer showed an even increased ΔEp = ~ 388 mV and HER2 antigen layer to ΔEp = 402 mV, Fig. 6B. The increase in ΔEp upon addition of the BSA, Trastuzumab and HER2 confirmed decreased conductivity. ΔEp increased with HER2 concentration, Table 1. The background current decreased with increase in the concentration of HER2, Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) characterization of the fabricated Immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrode surface was characterized at each step leading (as shown in Scheme 3) to the fabrication of the immunosensor using EIS, the obtained data is shown in Fig. 7 . The stepwise electrochemical response of each electrode is recorded in terms of Rct values. The Rct value is a measure of electron transfer resistance on an electrode, and increases with the decrease of the conductivity on an electrode surface. The Rct value for the BGCE was obtained to be 95 Ω, which increased to 291 Ω upon modification with the CoP-BNF (GCE/CoP-BNF). The increased resistance, which in turns means decreased electron transfer might be as a result of aggregation of the porphyrin framework thereby resulting in electrode passivation. The decrease in conductivity (increase in Rct) continued for GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs), with a Rct value of 566 Ω. An increase in Rct values upon addition of the Trastuzumab onto the electrode was observed (GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab) corresponding to the increase in ΔEp. A further increase in Rct values was seen when HER2 was added onto the electrode at varying concentrations; the Rct values increased with increased HER2 concentration, Table 1 and Table S1.

The SEM analysis of the bare and modified glassy carbon plate (GCP) of the various steps towards immunosensor fabrication was performed, Fig. 8 . A typical bare glassy carbon plate surface is shown in Fig. 8a; a clean grey surface is observed. The surface for GCP/CoP-BNF show coating of the carbon plate with white crystalline material which might be attributed to the CoP-BNF, Fig. 8b. The GCP/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs surface is shown in Fig. 8c; this surface is formed from the addition of the SNGQDs@AuNPs onto the GCP/CoP-BNF. A blanket of grey particles was formed on top of the previously formed white CoP-BNF particles, Fig. 8c.

The performance of the designed immunosensors towards different concentrations of HER2 was tested by recording EIS of PBS in 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− for the three electrodes GCE/CoP-BNF/Trasmatuzab (Fig. 9B), GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trasmatuzab (Fig. S4) and GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trasmatuzab (Fig. 9A). The regression plots Rct vs. log [HER2] were obtained from the data in Fig. 9 and S4. The limits of detection and sensitivity of the designed immunosensors were determined from the regression plots.

The LOD values were determines using the 3 s/m, where s is the standard deviation of Ret values obtained for PBS (without HER2) and (m) is the sensitivity and slope of the regression plot of EIS data obtained for the measurements of all three electrodes, Table 2 . The LOD values obtained for the designed immunosensors were GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/ Trastuzumab (0.0327 ng/mL), GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab (0.0454 ng/mL) and GCE/CoP-BNF/ Trastuzumab (0.1072 ng/mL), Tables 2 and 3 . Thus, the LOD for all values are less than the cut off level of HER2 concentration of 15 ng/mL in body serum, and as such the sensors are promising for the early detection of HER2 in early stage breast cancer detection. LOD for GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab is lower than for GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab, and GCE/CoP-BNF/ Trastuzumab hence showing the synergy between the components of the sensors. The performance of the current sensors (in terms of LOD) was compared to what has been reported in literature for the electrochemical detection of HER2. As shown in Table 2, it can be seen that the performance of the present sensors is better than literature reports in some cases [35–41]. Time for electrode preparation for GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab was lower than for some of the best performing electrodes in Table 2.

To test the regenerative performance of the designed immunosensor the solvent environment was altered, to overcome the attraction forces between the antibody and the HER2 antigen. Initially, all experiments were conducted in pH 7.4 media. The pH was now altered to acidic values for 15 min to allow for desorption of the HER2 from the antibody. The resulting electrode was rinsed thoroughly with pH 6 PBS buffer to restore the antibody to its native conformation represented as HER2-decoupled in Fig. 10A, this electrode now has the antibody, Trastuzumab (and no HER2). HER2 was then reintroduced to the Co-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab electrode to form Co-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab /HER2. This is represented as HER2 coupled in Fig. 10B. And the Rct obtained was comparable to that obtained for Co-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trastuzumab /HER2 in Fig. 9A). Three cycles were run of each of the electrodes, where the Rct values were about the same with RSD values of 5%, proving the efficiency of the regenerated sensor to be the same as the freshly prepared electrode.

In order to test the reproducibility of the designed immunosensors towards HER2 analyte, all experiments were run in triplicate with the RSD values were determined, Table 4 . The average (%RSD) for all three electrodes was less than 8%, GCE/CoP-NF/Trasmatuzab (6.7%), GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trasmatuzab (average = 7.3%) and GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs/Trasmatuzab (average = 2.1%) which is acceptable for a biosensor. The stability of the immunosensor was assessed by running EIS of the electrode in HER2, no significant change was observed over 21 days, stored at 4 °C. The accuracy of the method was tested, the concentrations of HER2 in the spiked serum samples from normal human that diluted 500-fold were measured, Table 4, Table S2. The percentage recoveries of the immunosensors GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs were better (74%–109%) compared to the GCE/SNGQDs@AuNPs (71% - 106%) and GCE/CoP-BNF (73% - 109%), all run in triplicates, Table 4, Table S2. The results showed the role of the synergistic effect of the CoP-BNF and SNGQDs@AuNPs combination, and its role in the detection of the HER2 analyte.

In this work novel impedimetric sensors based on carbon based GQDs and porphyrin binuclear framework were fabricated and investigated towards the detection of HER2. Graphene quantum dots were chosen due to their excessive structural functionality. The carboxylic groups allowed for covalent linkage to the amino containing Trasmatuzab via an amide bond. The S and N atoms were good host sites for gold nanoparticles on the surface to stabilize and enhance the GQDs electron conductivity. The CoP-BNF with its redox active cobalt center and carboxylic groups was ideal for antibody adsorption as well as signal amplification. The nanomaterials were explored individually and when combined towards the detection of HER2 via impedimetric methods. The SNGQDs@AuNPs nanomaterials was used to improve the properties of the CoP-BNF as a transducer and an immobilization platform for immunosensor design. The results obtained showed that the GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs (combination of the CoP-BNF and SNGQDs@AuNPs on the electrode) showed better sensitivity and limit of detection (0.0327 ng/mL) compared to the GCE/CoP-BNF (0.107 ng/mL) and GCE/SNGQDs (0.045 ng/mL). The applicability of the designed sensors was tested against real life human serums; the performance followed the trend of the results obtained for the HER2 in PBS, where the GCE/CoP-BNF/SNGQDs@AuNPs is the best. In addition, the obtained LOD values were lower than 15 ng/mL which is the cut off concentration of the HER2 protein in normal cells. The sensor also showed good reproducibility and stability, making it a potential candidate for HER2 sensor tool in breast cancer patients.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), and Rhodes University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2020.114908.
